questions.prompt: You ran `git reset --hard HEAD~5` and now can't find your work. How to recover?

choices.text: Use the reflog: `git reflog` shows every HEAD movement for the last ~90 days, including the position before the reset. Find the entry just before the reset (e.g., HEAD@{1}); checkout that commit (`git checkout HEAD@{1}`); create a branch to save it (`git switch -c recovered`). The reflog is Git's safety net — even 'lost' commits exist in the object store and are reachable via the reflog until garbage collection (~90 days). First thing to check whenever 'I lost a commit' happens. Saves work most teams assumed was gone forever.

questions.explanation: `git reflog` shows recent HEAD movements; checkout the previous position; create a branch to save the work.

questions.prompt: You committed an AWS secret key, even briefly. What now?

choices.text: Just remove the file in next commit.

ROTATE the credential immediately — treat it as compromised regardless of what you do to the repo. Bots scrape new commits within minutes; cached clones may have it; public mirrors may have it. Removing in next commit doesn't remove from history; history is permanent. Steps: (1) Generate new AWS key; revoke old; check CloudTrail for any unauthorized use during the window of exposure. (2) Add proper .gitignore for .env / config files. (3) Set up pre-commit secret scanning (gitleaks) so this can't repeat. (4) For high-stakes: rewrite history via git filter-repo to remove the file (helps clones from now on; doesn't help anything already cloned). GitHub's secret scanning notifies many providers (AWS auto-revokes some keys), but never count on that — rotation is your responsibility.

questions.explanation: ROTATE immediately; treat as compromised. History is permanent. Pre-commit hooks prevent recurrence.
